elements.sent-2411-005: Here is a bibliography with two entries marked up in the {\tt\string natbib style:.}
elements.sent-2411-006: The square-bracketed argument contains the "author" portion of the citation followed by the year in parentheses.
elements.sent-2411-007: The parentheses are important, so do not leave them out.
elements.sent-2411-008: Note that in the second {\tt\string\bibitem , the square-bracketed argument includes two author lists, a short version before the year and a long version after.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Authors should use this form of the markup if they wish to use the * forms of the {\tt\string\cite commands.}
elements.sent-2411-010: The two basic text citation commands are {\tt\string\citet and {\tt\string\citep .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: Use the asterisk (*) form of the cite commands to print the long version of the author lists for references that have been marked up in the manner of the "jon90" example.
elements.sent-2411-012: Here are some {\tt\string\citet and {\tt\string\citep examples taken from the {\tt\string natbib package documentation\footnote{\url{http://www.ctan.org/pkg/natbib .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: It is widely assumed that dust opacities in molecular clouds follow a power-law profile with an index, $\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-014: Recent studies of the Orion Molecular Cloud (OMC) 2/3 complex, however, show a flattening in the spectral energy distribution (SED) at $ \lambda > 2$ mm implying non-constant indices on scales $\gtrsim$ 0.08 pc.
elements.sent-2411-015: The origin of this flattening is not yet known but it may be due to the intrinsic properties of the dust grains or contamination from other sources of emission.
elements.sent-2411-016: We investigate the SED slopes in OMC 2/3 further using observations of six protostellar cores with NOEMA from 2.9 mm to 3.6 mm and ALMA-ACA in Band 4 (1.9 -- 2.1 mm) and Band 5 (1.6 -- 1.8 mm) on core and envelope scales of $\sim 0.02 - 0.08$ pc.
elements.sent-2411-017: We confirm flattened opacity indices between 2.9 mm and 3.6 mm for the six cores with $\beta \approx -0.16 - 1.45$, which are notably lower than the $\beta$ values of $> 1.3$ measured for these sources on $0.08$ pc scales from single-dish data.
elements.sent-2411-018: Four sources have consistent SED slopes between the ALMA data and the NOEMA data.
elements.sent-2411-019: We propose that these sources may have a significant fraction of emission coming from large dust grains in embedded disks, which biases the emission more at longer wavelengths.
elements.sent-2411-020: Two sources, however, had inconsistent slopes between the ALMA and NOEMA data, indicating different origins of emission.
elements.sent-2411-021: These results highlight how care is needed when combining multi-scale observations or extrapolating single-band observations to other wavelengths.
elements.sent-2411-022: The OMC 2/3 region within the Orion Molecular Cloud (OMC) complex contains a rich, nearby filamentary structure that enables detailed observations of various stages of star formation processes, making it an invaluable laboratory for studying the fundamental physics governing the birth of stars and planetary systems \citep[e.g.,][]{Chini1997,Peterson2005,Sadavoy2010,Stutz2015,Salji2015,Megeath2016,Hacar2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: The proximity and wealth of the young stellar content in OMC 2/3 allows researchers to map its star formation activity and connect that activity to the properties of the filament.
elements.sent-2411-024: Thermal dust emission is particularly useful as a tracer of molecular cloud mass and structure, despite gas being more abundant in the interstellar medium (ISM).
elements.sent-2411-025: First, thermal dust emission can be used to derive temperature and (column) density information.
elements.sent-2411-026: Second, dust is found on all scales, whereas emission from a single molecular species will trace gas at, e.g., a narrow range of densities, and thus will be sensitive to a limited range of physical scales \citep[e.g.,][]{Caselli2002,Crapsi2005,Ceccarelli2007,Andersson2015,Guillet2020,Liu2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-027: Dust opacity is an intrinsic property of dust grains that specify their ability to absorb light and ultimately re-radiate light.
elements.sent-2411-028: It is commonly described by a power-law profile of the form $\kappa_\nu \propto \nu^\beta$ at long wavelengths ($\lambda > 100 \ \mathrm{\mu m}$), but the slope of this power law varies with dust properties, particularly grain size \citep[e.g.,][]{Ossenkopf1994,D'Alessio2001, Ormel2011, Testi2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Nevertheless, many studies characterize dust opacity with fixed values of $\beta$.
elements.sent-2411-030: For instance, values of $\beta \approx 1.8 - 2$ are often used for dust opacity in molecular clouds and dense cores \citep[e.g.,][]{Ysard2012,juvela2018,Long2020 , whereas values of $\beta < 1$ are typical for protoplanetary disks \citep[e.g.,][]{Birnstiel2012,Andrews2018,Hendler2020,Tazzari2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Contrary to these expectations, multiple recent studies on the OMC 2/3 complex have reported conflicting results regarding the $\beta$ index.
elements.sent-2411-032: These low values are more similar to what is measured in disks, whereas they were measuring $\beta$ on core-scales and in filaments where steeper values are expected.
elements.sent-2411-033: Using Herschel data at $160-500 \, \mathrm{\mu m}$ and IRAM 30 m data at 2 mm to examine the larger-scale cloud, \citet{Sadavoy2016 found more typical values of $\beta$ in OMC 2/3.}
elements.sent-2411-034: Their SED fits gave $\beta > 1.2$ across all of OMC 2/3, with most of the filament exhibiting $\beta \approx 1.7-1.8$ on scales greater than 0.08 pc.
elements.sent-2411-035: Instead, their SEDs suggested that the SED flattens at $\lambda > 2$ mm.
elements.sent-2411-036: More recently, \citet{mason2020 confirmed the flatter spectral index at $\lambda > 2$ mm in OMC 2/3 using new 3 mm and 1 cm data from the GBT with Herschel observations.}
elements.sent-2411-037: Specifically, they examined SED using dust emission from slices through the filament, demonstrating elevated emission at $\lambda \gtrsim 3$ mm even toward starless cores and filamentary material on scales of $\sim 0.08$ pc.
elements.sent-2411-038: While studies such as \citet{Miotello2014 , \citet{Li2017 , and \citet{Galametz2019 have identified $\beta \sim 1$ in the envelopes of Class 0 young stellar objects (YSOs), these measurements are typically on scales $< 0.01$ pc ($<$ 2000 au), whereas the aforementioned OMC 2/3 studies find these values of $\beta$ over a much larger spatial scale of $\sim 0.08 - 1$ pc.}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Thus, OMC 2/3 shows a discrepancy in spectral slope on core scales.
elements.sent-2411-040: Moreover, following the approach by \citet{mason2020 , \citet{Lowe2022 showed similar flattened spectral slopes in slides through Orion A, Orion B, and Serpens, indicating that this discrepancy is not unique to OMC 2/3 and must be commonplace.}}
elements.sent-2411-041: Normally, flatter SEDs at long wavelengths can be indicative of different physical processes, such as free-free emission or Anomalous Microwave Emission (AME) \citep[e.g.,][]{Draine1998,Draine2011,Planck2016_diffuseforeground .}
elements.sent-2411-042: Nevertheless, neither of these processes appear to explain the flattened SED profiles \citep{mason2020, Lowe2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Free-free emission is typically associated with protostellar jets or winds \citep[e.g.][]{Anglada1995, Anglada1998, shang2004, Dicker2009 and is normally not typically seen on core scales $\gtrsim 0.1$ pc or toward starless cores and filaments.}
elements.sent-2411-044: AME is attributed to small dust grains that are spinning and is mostly detected on much larger (cloud) scales \citep[e.g.,][]{Ysard2011, Hoang2016, mason2020 than what has been studied.}
elements.sent-2411-045: To characterize the change in SED slope seen in OMC 2/3 better, we obtained Northern Extended Millimeter Array (NOEMA) data at 3 mm and Atacama Compact Array (ACA) Band 4 (137-153 GHz) and Band 5 (163-179 GHz) observations for six, bright protostellar cores in OMC 2/3 on $< 0.1$ pc scales.
elements.sent-2411-046: These cores all exhibited elevated 3 mm emission based on prior Herschel and GBT data \citep{Sadavoy2016, mason2020 , and they are all bright, compact, and isolated, making them the best targets to map the SED slope by imaging the basebands separately and to avoid confusion with extended emission or neighboring sources.}
elements.sent-2411-047: By comparing multiwavelength data on $<0.1$ pc scales, we aim to identify how the SED slope is changing over the critical wavelength range between 1 and 3 mm using data that capture similar scales.
elements.sent-2411-048: This paper is structured as follows: Section 2 presents the NOEMA and ALMA observations; Section 3 discusses the analysis and slope calculations; Section 4 presents the results; Section 5 discusses the individual sources; Section 6 explores several physical mechanisms that could explain the SED properties; finally, Section 7 summarizes the key results and interpretations, and presents our conclusions.
elements.sent-2411-049: We observed six dense cores within the OMC 2/3 filament with NOEMA in D-configuration with 10 antennas on 19 February 2019.
elements.sent-2411-050: Table \ref{tab:list of sources lists the six cores and their properties.}
elements.sent-2411-051: The correlator was set to 81-104 GHz, with a total bandwidth of 16 GHz split between four basebands with 2 MHz resolution and 1828 channels per baseband.
elements.sent-2411-052: Additionally, 11 smaller windows were included for higher spectral resolution, but these are not examined here.
elements.sent-2411-053: The total observing time was 1.18 hours with single pointings over a shared track.
elements.sent-2411-054: The observations used 3C84 for flux calibration and B0458-020 and B0539-057 for phase calibration.
elements.sent-2411-055: Phase and amplitude radio frequency (RF) calibration was performed by interpolating between channels, and a spectral index of -0.38 was adopted for the RF calibrator.
elements.sent-2411-056: To reduce effects from unstable weather conditions, initial scans were flagged to ensure accurate calibration unaffected by transient phase and amplitude fluctuations.
elements.sent-2411-057: Calibration was performed primarily on Antenna 1 before extending the solution to all receivers, ensuring consistency.
elements.sent-2411-058: Our NOEMA calibration involved both frequency and time domains, correcting instrumental effects through standard 30-80 degree phase thresholds for acceptable system deviation.
elements.sent-2411-059: The standard pipeline executed bandpass calibration and indicated consistent antenna response.
elements.sent-2411-060: The calibration uncertainties were estimated to be $<$ 40 degrees in phase rms, $<$ 21.6\% amplitude loss, $<$ 30\% in pointing error, and $<$ in 30\% focus error.
elements.sent-2411-061: After calibration, molecular line subtraction was performed before imaging.
elements.sent-2411-062: Lines were subtracted per source and baseband by flagging line channels.
elements.sent-2411-063: A filtering process then extracted line emission, generating a continuum UV-table for dirty image creation prior to cleaning.
elements.sent-2411-064: Protostellar classifications (column 4) and disk masses (columns 5 and 6) are taken from \citet{Tobin2020 , which presents ALMA 0.87 mm and VLA 9 mm observations.}
elements.sent-2411-065: The disk masses represent the summed total of all disks located within the NOEMA beam ($< 6\arcsec$) at the position of each source.
elements.sent-2411-066: The envelope masses (column 7) are taken from the Herschel Orion Protostellar Survey \citep[HOPS,][]{Furlan2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-067: NOEMA basebands are 4 GHz, ALMA basebands are 3.6 GHz.
elements.sent-2411-068: The ALMA data combine the two lower sideband spectral windows (Low) and two upper sideband spectral windows (High) for better sensitivity.
elements.sent-2411-069: For the ALMA data, the rms values will be 1.2 times higher for MMS9 and NW167 because of their primary beam corrections.
elements.sent-2411-070: We paired the ALMA and NOEMA data as follows: LSB-O with Band 4-Low, LSB-I with Band 4-High, USB-I with Band 5-Low, and USB-O with Band 5-High.
elements.sent-2411-071: To further enhance image accuracy, phase only self-calibration was conducted as the target signal-to-noise ratios (SNRs) were sufficiently high ($>100$).
elements.sent-2411-072: We performed one round of phase only self-calibration with interval of 45s.
elements.sent-2411-073: Subsequently, another round of cleaning was carried out with a reduced noise threshold of 0.2 m\Jybeam.
elements.sent-2411-074: We employed the MAPPING method in GILDAS \citep[e.g.,][]{GILDAS for data imaging with a map size of 128 by 128 pixels, corresponding to 92.16\arcsec\ by 92.16\arcsec, and 0.72\arcsec\ pixels.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Since the observations had such high SNR, we imaged each of the four basebands separately to infer the SED slopes better.
elements.sent-2411-076: Figure \ref{fig:source_gallery shows images of all our targets at 86.78 GHz, corresponding to the inner lower side band (LSB-I).}
elements.sent-2411-077: The targets are singular except for FIR6B where a second compact source is seen south of the main target.
elements.sent-2411-078: Several sources also show extended emission like MMS9 and FIR2.
elements.sent-2411-079: The measured properties of each compact source are detailed in Section 3.
elements.sent-2411-080: We observed the entire OMC 2/3 filament in Band 4 (137-153 GHz) and Band 5 (163-179 GHz) with the Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array (ALMA) ACA from October 2017 to December 2017.
elements.sent-2411-081: The region was divided into two mosaics with one centered on OMC 2 (area of 3.2 $\times$ 7.0 arcmin) and the other on OMC 3 (area 3.7 $\times$ 7.5 arcmin in Band 4 and 3.4 $\times$ 7.4 arcmin in Band 5) for a total observing time of 9.53 hours.
elements.sent-2411-082: The Band 4 and Band 5 datasets were calibrated through standard procedures from the North American ALMA Science Center (NAASC).
elements.sent-2411-083: The flux calibrator was J0522-3627, and the phase calibrator was J0541-0211 for the Band 4 data and J0542-0913 for the Band 5 data.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\textwidth,height=9cm]{figures/source_gallery.png} \caption{Cleaned NOEMA images of all six protostellar cores in OMC 2/3 for the LSB-I baseband at a central frequency of 86.78157 GHz. {Contours represent a 5$\sigma$ level.} The synthesized beam is plotted in the bottom left corner. {These maps represent the unsmoothed NOEMA observations.}} \label{fig:source_gallery}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[trim=1.0cm 2mm 1mm 1mm,clip=true,width=0.97\textwidth]{figures/omcmap.png} \caption{Cleaned continuum emission maps of OMC 2 (left) and OMC 3 (right) at Band 4-High (150.9918 GHz) from ALMA/ACA observations as an example. {These maps correspond to the unsmoothed mosaics with a uv-cut of 5 k$\lambda$. A 5$\sigma$ contour is overlaid on the maps.} The six target sources are labeled and the synthesized beam is shown in the lower-left corner. {The red boxes represent the regions used for flux extraction with imfit in CASA.}} \label{figomcmap}
elements.sent-2411-084: We employed the tclean algorithm in the CASA software \citep{McMullin2007 , utilizing the Hogbom deconvolver.}
elements.sent-2411-085: Prior to cleaning, the ALMA data were inspected to identify any channels that could be contaminated by line emission and such channels were flagged.
elements.sent-2411-086: We applied an iterative approach for phase-only self-calibration, similar to the method described by \citet{Ginsburg2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-087: For all cleaning rounds, we used Briggs weighting with a robust parameter of 0.5 to optimize both sensitivity and resolution \citep{Briggs1995,Briggs1999 .}
elements.sent-2411-088: Since our targets are bright, we imaged each sideband separately to obtain a better measure of the spectral index between 136 GHz and 177 GHz.
elements.sent-2411-089: To ensure a consistent comparison between the ALMA and NOEMA data, we generated images from the ACA visibilities with a uv range cutoff of uv $> 5~k\lambda$, which corresponds to spatial scales of $\lesssim$ 40 arcsec.
elements.sent-2411-090: This choice was motivated by the minimum baseline length of approximately 5 k$\lambda$ observed in our NOEMA data.
elements.sent-2411-091: The purpose of specifying the uv range was to enable the characterization of the SED slope for each target using data that trace the same spatial scales.
elements.sent-2411-092: Table \ref{obs_table gives the ALMA synthesized beam information and map sensitivities for the ``Low'' and ``High'' Band 4 and 5 data following this uv cut (columns 4 -- 6) and the smoothed data used in the following analysis (columns 7 -- 9).}
elements.sent-2411-093: Figure \ref{figomcmap shows the clean Band 4 mosaic maps of OMC 2 and OMC 3 as an example.}
elements.sent-2411-094: To construct SEDs for each source, we need the total fluxes across the multiple datasets.
elements.sent-2411-095: While we used a uv-cut to ensure that the ALMA-ACA data and NOEMA data cover similar spatial scales, the resulting synthesized beams differ by factors of 2.5 -- 2.7 in area.
elements.sent-2411-096: We therefore smoothed the datasets to better match in resolution.
elements.sent-2411-097: For simplicity, we smoothed the ALMA and NOEMA data by the synthesized beam of the other, matching the highest to lowest frequencies for each pair (e.g., LSB-O with Band 4-Low, LSB-I with Band 4-High, etc.).
elements.sent-2411-098: The resulting smoothed beam resolutions are given in columns 7 -- 9 in Table \ref{obs_table .}
elements.sent-2411-099: Therefore, the smoothed maps consistently cover scales from 0.02 pc -- 0.08 pc.
elements.sent-2411-100: We stress that unlike prior studies which combined data with different resolutions and different degrees of filtering, these observations should be consistently tracing emission from the envelope and core over the same spatial scales.
elements.sent-2411-101: The integrated flux of each source is extracted using the imfit task in CASA.
elements.sent-2411-102: This task fits the source emission with two-dimensional Gaussian functions in the image plane, allowing us to estimate source parameters {such as the position, size, and total flux of each source.}
elements.sent-2411-103: For the NOEMA data, we used a $30\times30$ pixel box centered in the individual pointing map for all of the sources.
elements.sent-2411-104: For the ALMA data, we generated boxes {with a box size of about $50\times50$ pixels centered on each source in the mosaics.}
elements.sent-2411-105: In both cases, we fitted both a Gaussian and a background level, {with the background being treated as a free parameter (dooff = True) since there was still extended emission around the sources.}
elements.sent-2411-106: Table \ref{fitting_result gives the source fitting results from imfit.}
elements.sent-2411-107: For each source, we give the size and position angle (deconvolved from the beam) and total flux at each observed frequency.
elements.sent-2411-108: Errors are from the fit alone and do not include calibration uncertainties, {which we estimate to be 10\% .}
elements.sent-2411-109: We used the NOEMA and ALMA multi-wavelength maps to construct SED profiles for the six protostellar cores along the OMC 2/3 filament.
elements.sent-2411-111: This model is generally seen as an excellent description of dust emission at millimeter and submillimeter wavelengths, with typical values of $1.5 < \beta < 2.5$ on filament and cloud scales \citep[e.g.,][]{Goldsmith1997, Sadavoy2013, Dib2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-112:
For low frequency emission (long wavelengths) on the Rayleigh-Jeans tail, $h
u/kT_d \ll 1$ and Equation 1 simplifies to:  egin{equation}I_{
u,dust} \propto 
u^{2+ eta} \equiv 
u^\alpha \label{MBB2}\end{equation} where $\alpha = 2+  eta$ is called the spectral index.
elements.sent-2411-113: Since all our observations are at low frequencies of $\nu < 180$ GHz and {we do not have robust constraints on the dust temperature for these targets on the observed scales , we used Equation \ref{MBB2 to measure the spectral slope, $\alpha$ and infer $\beta$.}}
elements.sent-2411-114: We implemented a Monte Carlo Markov Chain (MCMC) code to measure $\alpha$ from the SED slope.
elements.sent-2411-115: To optimize the fitting process, we utilized the emcee code \citep{emcee2013 with an assumed, simple power-law model of $S_\nu = A\nu^{\alpha}$.}
elements.sent-2411-116: To test how the slope changes for each source, we fitted power-law functions to the NOEMA data and ALMA-ACA data separately.
elements.sent-2411-117: When running emcee, we employed uniform priors on the parameter $\alpha$, with bounds set as $-5.0 < \alpha < 5.0$.
elements.sent-2411-118: We also performed emcee with 32 walkers, enabling the walkers to rapidly explore the entire posterior distribution.
elements.sent-2411-119: Since the flux errors reported in Table \ref{fitting_result do not include flux calibration uncertainties, we further used a Monte Carlo approach to better determine the power-law slope error from the ALMA Band 4 and Band 5 data, which each have estimated flux calibration uncertainties of 10\%.}
elements.sent-2411-120: We used the Monte Carlo method to determine the uncertainty in the SED slopes from flux calibration errors because these errors are correlated for Band 4 and Band 5.
elements.sent-2411-121: We used a random selector to generate two sets of 5000 samples from a Gaussian distribution with mean 1 and standard deviation of 0.1 to represent the 10\% flux calibration uncertainty in each band.
elements.sent-2411-122: We applied the randomly-selected values to the observed data and re-fitted the power law slopes.
elements.sent-2411-123: We then used the \nth{16 and \nth{84 percentiles of best-fit slopes to represent the error.}}
elements.sent-2411-124: For the NOEMA data, since the data were taken simultaneously with the same flux calibration, the flux calibration errors will not affect the slope.
elements.sent-2411-125: Figure \ref{fig:omc_result shows the SEDs of all sources using the NOEMA and ALMA data from 83 GHz to 177 GHz ($\sim 1.7 - 3.6$ mm).}
elements.sent-2411-126: Plotted also are power-law best-fit slopes for the ALMA data alone (dashed lines for ALMA best fit and yellow area for the Monto Carlo uncertainties) the NOEMA data alone (dotted lines), and the best-fit modified black body function obtained using single-dish data ($\beta$-SD; solid lines) from \citet{Sadavoy2016 using data from Herschel and IRAM (0.16 -- 2 mm).}
elements.sent-2411-127: Broadly, the NOEMA data spanning 82.7 -- 102.3 GHz (2.9 -- 3.6 mm) show elevated fluxes relative to the single-dish modified black body function (solid line), which is consistent with \citet{Sadavoy2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-128: It is interesting that the NOEMA data are still considerably elevated considering that these data are from interferomtery which filters out emission on scales $> 40$\arcsec, whereas the single-dish data, including the prior GBT data at 3 mm, are sensitive to emission on larger scales.
elements.sent-2411-129: This excess flux at $\lambda \gtrsim 2.9$ mm suggests that the NOEMA data (and similarly the GBT data) are dominated by emission coming from a different origin than the emission captured by the single-dish data at 0.16 -- 2 mm.
elements.sent-2411-130: The ALMA data yield more mixed results.
elements.sent-2411-131: In some cases, the ALMA data are elevated over the single-dish modified blackbody function (solid line), whereas in other cases it is below or in agreement with the single-dish trend.
elements.sent-2411-132: As with the NOEMA observations, the ALMA data are filtered on scales $> 40$\arcsec, so we would expect that emission at comparable wavelengths (e.g., the single-dish data is constrained by emission at 145 GHz (2 mm) at 22\arcsec\ resolution from the IRAM 30m telescope) to be less for the ALMA data.
elements.sent-2411-133: Nevertheless, we caution against over-interpreting the difference in flux between ALMA and the modified blackbody fit from the single-dish data as differences in source extraction and map resolution could also be a factor.
elements.sent-2411-134: Table \ref{table_SEDresult lists the spectral indices ($\alpha$) and subsequent dust opacity indices ($\beta$) for each target as measured from the NOEMA and ALMA data alone.}
elements.sent-2411-135: We fit separate slopes for the ALMA and NOEMA data due to evidence of a break (flattening) in the power-law profile at $\lambda \gtrsim 3$ mm.
elements.sent-2411-136: Looking at the sample as a whole, we find that (1) the NOEMA and ALMA slopes tend to be shallower than the single-dish modified blackbody fits and (2) the NOEMA and ALMA slopes themselves tend to agree within $1 \sigma$ errors of each other.
elements.sent-2411-137: The median $\beta$ indices are 0.74, 0.68, and 1.8 for the NOEMA, ALMA, and single-dish data, respectively, where the NOEMA and ALMA data have $\beta < 1.3$ in most cases and the single-dish data have $\beta \gtrsim 1.3$.
elements.sent-2411-138: The exceptions to both of these trends are with FIR2 and MMS6, where the NOEMA and ALMA slopes differ by 2$\sigma$ and we measure a steeper slope for either NOEMA or ALMA compared to the single-dish fits.
elements.sent-2411-139: These distinctions suggest that FIR2 and MMS6 may be unusual sources among the entire sample.
elements.sent-2411-140: We discuss the individual sources in more detail in the next section.
elements.sent-2411-141: We note that our analysis used Equation \ref{MBB2 (power-law fits to the SEDs) rather than modified blackbody fit (e.g., Equation \ref{MBB ).}}
elements.sent-2411-142: For emission on the Rayleigh-Jeans tail, these two approaches should be equivalent.
elements.sent-2411-143: To verify this assumption, we calculated $\alpha$ from Equation \ref{MBB assuming a typical dust temperature of 25 K \citep[e.g.,][]{Sadavoy2016, Friesen2017 and fixed $\beta = 1$ to represent the typical values in OMC 2/3.}}
elements.sent-2411-144: The inferred $\beta$ values derived from the $\beta = \alpha - 2$ assumption compared to the more accurate modified blackbody fit were approximately 15\% and 10\% underestimated for the ALMA and NOEMA data, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-145: These differences are less than the errors reported in Table \ref{table_SEDresult and therefore our assumption does not greatly affect our results.}
elements.sent-2411-146: We nevertheless prefer to use a power-law fit over a modified blackbody fit because the temperatures themselves are unknown at these scales.
elements.sent-2411-147: Since we are targeting dust emission on $\sim$ 0.02 -- 0.08 pc ($\sim$ 4000 -- 16000 au) scales around luminous protostars, the dust temperatures could be much higher than the assumed 25 K (which was taken from single dish data that encompass larger scales).
elements.sent-2411-148: For these reasons, the spectral indices will better match the true modified blackbody slopes over assuming an incorrect temperature.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=13.5cm]{figures/finalResult_smooth_NOEMA_ALMA_.png} \caption{SEDs for the six sources with best-fit power-law slopes for the ALMA data (dashed) and NOEMA data (dotted). The yellow areas show the uncertainty on the ALMA slope from 10\% flux calibration errors. Solid lines show the modified blackbody functions from single-dish data (Herschel at 0.16 -- 0.5 mm and IRAM+GISMO at 2 mm) from \citet{Sadavoy2016}.} \label{fig:omc_result}
elements.sent-2411-149: Below we discuss the results for each source.
elements.sent-2411-150: See Figure \ref{fig:omc_result and Table \ref{table_SEDresult for the SEDs and best-fit slopes.}}
elements.sent-2411-151: FIR2 is a deeply embedded, low-mass protostar driving an outflow \citep{Takahashi2008,Tanabe2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-152: It has a moderate luminosity of approximately $1.3 \, L_{\odot}$ and is surrounded by a flattened, relatively dense, infalling envelope with an estimated mass infall rate of $7.6 \times 10^{-6 \, M_{\odot \, \text{yr ^{-1}$ \citep{Poteet2011 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-153: FIR2 is the first protostar to show unambiguous evidence of crystalline silicates in absorption, likely due to thermal processing, with temperatures reaching 1000 K \citep{Poteet2011 , and significant free-free emission \citep{Bouvier2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-154: The SED for FIR2 is unusual among the other sources.
elements.sent-2411-155: It has the flattest slope from the NOEMA data and the only source with $\alpha < 2$ ($\beta < 0$).
elements.sent-2411-156: Such a shallow slope at NOEMA frequencies is consistent with a high fraction of free-free emission \citep{Bouvier2021 (see Section \ref{subsec:FreeFree for further discussion).}}
elements.sent-2411-157: FIR2 also has the steepest slope measured from the ALMA data.
elements.sent-2411-158: The slopes from the NOEMA and ALMA measurements disagree at a 2$\sigma$ level, which suggests that the ALMA and NOEMA data are tracing different material over the same scales.
elements.sent-2411-159: Instead, the ALMA slope is more consistent with and even steeper than the modified blackbody fit from the single-dish ($\beta$-SD) data.

elements.sent-2411-223: In the following sections, we investigate potential explanations for the changing SED slopes, encompassing various factors, namely, free-free emission, AME, and disk contamination.
elements.sent-2411-224: Additionally, we delve into the consideration of complex dust grain opacity functions.
elements.sent-2411-225: These considerations aim to provide a comprehensive understanding of the underlying processes that can contribute to the flattened SED profiles and the associated variations in fluxes at longer wavelengths of protostellar cores within the OMC 2/3 filamentary region.

elements.sent-2411-236: Thermal bremsstrahlung, commonly known as ``free-free'' emission, involves the emission of photons by accelerated charged particles and occupies a frequency range between synchrotron and thermal dust emission, typically spanning from 10 GHz to 100 GHz \citep[e.g.,][]{Draine2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-237: Extended thermal free–free is more dominant in ionized regions of high-mass star formation like OMC 1 \citep[e.g.,][]{Dicker2009 , whereas OMC 2/3 lacks massive stars or star clusters \citep[e.g.,][]{Nielbock2003 .}}

elements.sent-2411-253: The presence of AME has emerged as a potential explanation for the flattened slope observed in the SED at $<150$ GHz ($>2$ mm) \citep[e.g.,][]{kogut1996,Leitch1997,Oliveira-Costa1997 .}
elements.sent-2411-254: It is predominantly attributed to electric dipole radiation emanating from small, charged spinning dust grains \citep[e.g.,][]{Draine1998, Hoang2016 .}
elements.sent-2411-255: While alternative models involving magnetic dipole emission from large ferrous dust grains or spinning nano-silicates have been proposed, the most compelling explanation for AME lies in electric dipole emission from ultrasmall grains \citep[e.g.,][]{Draine1999,Hoang2016,Greaves2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-256: Its typical peak frequencies usually fall within the 20-60 GHz range, where the peak can vary depending on local physical conditions, including grain size distribution, radiation intensity, dipole moment of the emitting grains, number density of grains, and ionization fractions of hydrogen and carbon \citep[e.g.,][]{Finkbeiner2004,Planck2011,Planck2015 .}
elements.sent-2411-257: To explore AME in different environments, \citet{mason2020 utilized the SPDUST2 \citep[e.g.][]{Ali2009,Silsbee2011 code to derive representative AME spectra, encompassing both diffuse molecular clouds and dark clouds.}}
elements.sent-2411-258: Upon comparing these AME models with actual observed data from the OMC 2/3 region, however, the models didn't match the observed data.
elements.sent-2411-259: Our SED slopes over the NOEMA frequencies support the idea that AME is not a major factor.
elements.sent-2411-260: We see no evidence of a turnover in the spectral indices such that flux increases with decreasing frequency (e.g., $S_{\nu \propto \nu^\alpha$ where $\alpha < 0$), which is expected for AME which peaks at frequencies below the observed NOEMA frequencies of 80 -- 100 GHz.}
elements.sent-2411-261: While we cannot rule out smaller contributions from AME without further investigation and more detailed modeling, we can conclude that AME is not a dominant feature at these scales.
elements.sent-2411-262: Fractions greater than 100\% are represented by 100\%.. \end{deluxetable*.}
elements.sent-2411-263: Protoplanetary disks around young protostars contain dust grains that can range in size from sub-micron to millimetre scales \citep[e.g.,][]{Villenave2020,Villenave2022, miotello2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-264: Emission at millimetre wavelengths is sensitive to radiation from larger dust grains, which are expected to be biased to the mass distribution within the disk \citep[e.g.,][]{Tobin2020 .}
elements.sent-2411-265: We determined the contribution of dust emission from the embedded protostellar disks within each source to determine whether or not these could be biasing the NOEMA and ALMA fluxes and ascertain whether disk dust emission can explain the elevated flux densities detected in some sources.
elements.sent-2411-266: For simplicity, we extrapolated ALMA observations at 340 GHz (0.87 mm) with 40 au resolution from \citet{Tobin2020 using a power-law relationship, $S_{\nu \sim \nu^\alpha$ to infer the contribution of disk emission.}}
elements.sent-2411-267: We then calculated the fraction of the observed NOEMA and ALMA fluxes that could be attributed to disk emission following, \begin{equation}Fraction=\frac{S_{disk,e}}{S_{obs}}= \frac{S_{disk} \times \left(\frac{\nu_e}{338.5\ \mathrm{GHz}}\right)^\alpha}{S_{obs}} \label{disk_fraction}\end{equation} where $S_{disk,e}$ is the extrapolated disk flux at an extrapolated frequency of $\nu_e$, $S_{disk}$ is the disk flux from \citet{Tobin2020 observed at 338.5 GHz\footnote{We adopted a frequency of 338.5 GHz, corresponding to the average of the center frequencies of the two continuum sidebands in \citet{Tobin2020 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-268: We adopted two different spectral indices, $\alpha = 2$ and $\alpha = 3$, corresponding to values of $\beta =$ 0 or 1, which are representative of typical values in disks \citep[e.g.,][]{Tychoniec2020,miotello2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-269: Therefore, values of $\alpha =2$ likely represents an upper limit on disk contamination and $\alpha = 3$ are a lower limit.
elements.sent-2411-270: To quantify the contribution of disk emission to the total flux for each protostellar core, we summed the contributions of all disks within the beam area of the NOEMA observations for each source.
elements.sent-2411-271: For example, four disks were summed for MMS9, while two disks were summed for MMS7.
elements.sent-2411-272: Once we estimated the flux contributions from the disks, we compared these measurements to the observed fluxes for each target.
elements.sent-2411-273: Table \ref{diskinfo presents the estimated fraction of observed emission that could be from disk contamination at each frequency band.}
elements.sent-2411-274: Table \ref{diskinfo gives the outcomes from our measurements of potential disk contamination.}
elements.sent-2411-275: These results suggest that most sources could have substantial contamination from disks, especially for low values of $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-276: Such low values of $\alpha$ are expected for optically thick disks or very large dust grains \citep[e.g.,][]{Carrasco-Gonzalez2019,Tychoniec2020,miotello2022 .}
elements.sent-2411-277: From \citet{Tobin2020 , however, most of the sources likely have spectral indices steeper than 2.}
elements.sent-2411-278: The only exception is MMS6, which is more likely consistent with $\alpha = 3$, in agreement with previous measurements of $\beta \approx 0.5-1$ from \citet{Takahashi2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-279: Therefore, we consider $\alpha = 3$ for MMS6 and a value between $\alpha = 2$ and $\alpha = 3$ for the remaining targets in the following analysis.
elements.sent-2411-280: We find that only for FIR2 ($\alpha = 2 - 3$) and MMS6 ($\alpha =3$, disk contamination is likely minimal.

elements.sent-2411-291: These sources also have consistent (within 1$\sigma$) slopes between the ALMA and NOEMA data, suggesting that the common mechanism to explain their dust emission may be disk emission.

elements.sent-2411-295: We also caution that we do not see strong evidence of corresponding emission excesses at 140 -- 180 GHz for these sources, which would be expected if the ALMA/NOEMA fluxes are elevated over the single-dish fluxes due to biases from disk emission.
elements.sent-2411-296: This analysis implies that the disks within some sources could possess enough mass to exert contamination on much larger scales ($>0.02$ pc or $> 4000$ au) than the disks themselves.
elements.sent-2411-297: Such contamination could also impact the single-dish GBT data, which have a comparable resolution to the smoothed NOEMA data and show similar fluxes as the NOEMA data (see Figure \ref{fig:NOEMA_MUSTANG_result ).}
elements.sent-2411-298: This result indicates that care is needed when interpreting higher resolution, low frequency observations either from single-dish telescopes or interferometers for protostars with embedded disks.
elements.sent-2411-299: An additional explanation for the flattened slopes observed in the SED profiles could be attributed to the intrinsic properties of the dust grains themselves.
elements.sent-2411-300: The formation of millimeter and sub-millimeter sized dust grains in the dense Orion cores, can create a flat spectral index compared to normal ISM dust grains \citep[e.g.,][]{Miettinen2012,Schnee2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-301: The large grains could account for the low $\beta$ values measured.
elements.sent-2411-302: As such, the elevated fluxes observed at $<150$ GHz may be explained by a change in the dust opacity slope, where the true distribution could follow a broken power-law with a flatter slope at $\nu < 150$ GHz ($\lambda > 2$ mm).
elements.sent-2411-303: The formation of such large dust grains in protostellar envelopes is possible due to rapid aggregation processes occurring before young stellar objects enter the Class II stage.
elements.sent-2411-304: Early grain growth is supported by evidence of micron-sized grains in dense cores and millimeter-sized grains in Class 0 and I objects as evidenced by flat SED profiles \citep[e.g.,][]{Pagani2011, Miotello2014,Galametz2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-305: Nevertheless, we caution that from our aforementioned disk contamination study, large dust grains seen on envelope-scales toward unresolved disks could be biased by the disks and not the envelopes.
elements.sent-2411-306: Modeling disk and envelope emission using visibilities over images would be necessary to break this degeneracy \citep[e.g., following][]{Miotello2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-307: Alternatively, sophisticated models derived from laboratory measurements of amorphous dust grains have produced flattened spectral slopes at millimeter wavelengths \citep[e.g.,][]{Boudet2005,meny2007,Coupeaud2011,Paradis2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-308: This trend primarily stems from two-level system fluctuations occurring within the dust grains.
elements.sent-2411-309: The model proposed by \citet{Paradis2011 was able to partially explain the elevated emission at 100 GHz from the GBT, but it under predicts the 30 GHz fluxes \citep[e.g.,][]{mason2020 .}}
elements.sent-2411-310: Further comprehensive studies are required to gain a deeper understanding of the complex behavior of dust grains at millimeter wavelengths.
elements.sent-2411-311: This study investigated an unexpected flux increase at 90 GHz in the OMC 2/3 region first noted by \citet{Schnee2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-312: We used multi-wavelength observations from NOEMA and ALMA to characterize the SED profile of six targets in the OMC 2/3 filamentary region to identify potential contributing factors to explain the change in SED slope at $< 100$ GHz.
elements.sent-2411-314: NOEMA slopes confirm the flatter profiles at frequencies $< 150$ GHz (wavelengths $>2$ mm).
elements.sent-2411-315: All sources except MMS6 give flatter SED slopes between 82.7 GHz and 102.3 GHz (2.9 -- 3.7 mm) compared to modified blackbody fits using single dish data (from 0.16 -- 2.0 mm).
elements.sent-2411-316: The ALMA slopes show a range of behaviors.
elements.sent-2411-317: FIR6B, MMS7, MMS9, and NW167 all show SEDs where the ALMA and NOEMA slopes are in agreement, indicating that the two instruments are tracing the same continuum emission mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-318: By contrast, FIR2 and MMS6 have discrepant ($>2 \sigma$) slopes between the ALMA and NOEMA data, suggesting that they are tracing different types of emission.
elements.sent-2411-319: Free-free emission from bremsstrahlung and anomalous microwave emission (AME) are both likely insufficient to explain the change in slope.
elements.sent-2411-320: Contamination from disk emission could be up to 70\% in FIR6B and MMS7, but the disk contributions were $\lesssim$ 40\% in FIR2, MMS6, MMS9, and NW167.
elements.sent-2411-321: Only for FIR2 could we rule out disk contamination.
elements.sent-2411-322: Therefore, the flattened slopes could be explained by contributions from disk emission for some protostars but not for all of them.
elements.sent-2411-323: Our analysis suggests that disk contamination could be a non-negligible factor on envelope and core scales, including high resolution single-dish telescopes at low frequencies (e.g., GBT).
elements.sent-2411-324: Multi-wavelength studies spanning a range of spatial scales and frequencies may need to take into account multiple dust components to accurately infer the SED shape and measure dust properties.
elements.sent-2411-325: Future work will be necessary to understand better the SEDs of these sources and to model their grain properties.
elements.sent-2411-326: While this study focuses specifically on Orion cores and examining individual protostars, \citet{Lowe2022 shows that elevated fluxes at $< 100$ GHz frequencies are seen throughout Orion A, Orion B, and Serpens on cloud (120\arcsec) resolutions.}
elements.sent-2411-327: Therefore, this change in SED slope may arise from emission origins that are on larger scales than disks or protostellar jets.
elements.sent-2411-328: Understanding the true shape of the SED over different scales and the origins of any changes in slope are necessary to elucidate the behaviour of dust in the ISM.
elements.sent-2411-329: Moreover, such models will have broader implications for interpreting dust emission and accurately measuring masses in molecular clouds and cores.

elements.sent-2411-340: High-resolution ALMA CO ($J=2\text{- 1$) observations reveal a velocity gradient along the short axis of the jet, with the launching radius estimated between $2.18$ and $2.96 \, \text{au}$, further supporting the disk wind hypothesis \citep{Matsushita2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-341: Table \ref{FF displays their estimated free-free emission fractions at 32.9 GHz based on the analysis of \citet{Bouvier2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-342: Specifically, Table \ref{FF compares the $\beta$ values obtained here from our NOEMA data (82.7 -- 102.3 GHz) with the corresponding measured $\beta$ index from \citet{Bouvier2021 using pairs of frequencies between 32.9 GHz and 333 GHz and a set of assumed temperatures.}}
elements.sent-2411-343: Early grain growth is supported by evidence of micron-sized grains in dense cores and millimeter-sized grains in Class 0 and I objects as evidenced by flat SED profiles \citep[e.g.,][]{Pagani2011, Schnee2014, Miotello2014,Galametz2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-344: Nevertheless, we caution that from our aforementioned disk contamination study, large dust grains seen on envelope-scales toward unresolved disks could be biased by the disks.
elements.sent-2411-345: Therefore, modeling disk and envelope emission using visibilities over images would be necessary to break this degeneracy \citep[e.g., following][]{Miotello2014, Galametz2019, Cacciapuoti2023 .}
